rule: Countries should identify, assess, and understand the money laundering and terrorist financing risks for the country, and should take action, including designating an authority or mechanism to coordinate actions to assess risks, and apply resources, aimed at ensuring the risks are mitigated effectively.
nlc: Verify that countries identify, assess, and understand the money laundering and terrorist financing risks for the country, and take action, including designating an authority or mechanism to coordinate actions to assess risks, and apply resources, aimed at ensuring the risks are mitigated effectively.

rule: The risk-based approach allows countries, within the framework of the FATF requirements, to adopt a more flexible set of measures, in order to target their resources more effectively and apply preventive measures that are proportionate to the nature of risks, in order to focus their efforts in the most effective way.
nlc: Verify that countries, within the framework of the FATF requirements, adopt a more flexible set of measures to target their resources more effectively and apply preventive measures that are proportionate to the nature of risks, in order to focus their efforts in the most effective way.

rule: The FATF Standards comprise the Recommendations themselves and their Interpretive Notes, together with the applicable definitions in the Glossary.
nlc: Verify that the FATF Standards comprise the Recommendations themselves and their Interpretive Notes, together with the applicable definitions in the Glossary.

rule: The measures set out in the FATF Standards should be implemented by all members of the FATF and the FSRBs, and their implementation is assessed rigorously through Mutual Evaluation processes, and through the assessment processes of the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank  on the basis of the FATF’s common assessment methodology.
nlc: Verify that the measures set out in the FATF Standards are implemented by all members of the FATF and the FSRBs, and that their implementation is assessed rigorously through Mutual Evaluation processes, and through the assessment processes of the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank on the basis of the FATF’s common assessment methodology.

rule: These examples are not mandatory elements of the FATF Standards, and are included for guidance only.
nlc: Verify that these examples are not mandatory elements of the FATF Standards and are included for guidance only.

rule: These other documents are not mandatory for assessing compliance with the Standards, but countries may find it valuable to have regard to them when considering how best to implement the FATF Standards.
nlc: Verify that other documents are not mandatory for assessing compliance with the Standards.

rule: Going forward and in accordance with its mandate, the FATF will continue to consider changes to the standards, as appropriate, in light of new information regarding emerging threats and vulnerabilities to the global financial system.
nlc: Verify that, in accordance with its mandate, the FATF continues to consider changes to the standards, as appropriate, in light of new information regarding emerging threats and vulnerabilities to the global financial system.

rule: The FATF calls upon all countries to implement effective measures to bring their national systems for combating money laundering, terrorist financing and the financing of proliferation into compliance with the revised FATF Recommendations.
nlc: Verify that all countries have implemented effective measures to bring their national systems for combating money laundering, terrorist financing and the financing of proliferation into compliance with the revised FATF Recommendations.

rule: Countries should apply a risk-based approach (RBA) to ensure that measures to prevent or mitigate money laundering and terrorist financing are proportionate to the risks identified.
nlc: Verify that countries apply a risk-based approach (RBA) to ensure that measures to prevent or mitigate money laundering and terrorist financing are proportionate to the risks identified.
sql: SELECT * FROM samld r1 WHERE r1.Is_Laundering is null;

rule: This approach should be an essential foundation to efficient allocation of resources across the anti-money laundering and countering the financing of terrorism (AML/CFT) regime and the implementation of risk-based measures throughout the FATF Recommendations.
nlc: Verify that this approach is an essential foundation to efficient allocation of resources across the anti-money laundering and countering the financing of terrorism (AML/CFT) regime and the implementation of risk-based measures throughout the FATF Recommendations.

rule: Based on that assessment, countries should apply a risk-based approach (RBA) to ensure that measures to prevent or mitigate money laundering and terrorist financing are proportionate to the risks identified.
nlc: Verify that countries apply a risk-based approach (RBA) to ensure that measures to prevent or mitigate money laundering and terrorist financing are proportionate to the risks identified.
sql: SELECT * FROM samld r1 WHERE r1.Is_Laundering is null;

rule: This approach should be an essential foundation to efficient allocation of resources across the anti-money laundering and countering the financing of terrorism (AML/CFT) regime and the implementation of risk-based measures throughout the FATF Recommendations.
nlc: Verify that this approach is an essential foundation to efficient allocation of resources across the anti-money laundering and countering the financing of terrorism (AML/CFT) regime and the implementation of risk-based measures throughout the FATF Recommendations.

rule: Where countries identify higher risks, they should ensure that their AML/CFT regime adequately addresses such risks.
nlc: Verify that for every country that identifies higher risks, its AML/CFT regime adequately addresses those risks.

rule: Where countries identify lower risks, they should allow and encourage simplified measures as appropriate.
nlc: Verify that where countries identify lower risks, they allow and encourage simplified measures as appropriate.

rule: Countries should also identify, assess, and understand the proliferation financing risks for the country.
nlc: Verify that countries identify, assess, and understand the proliferation financing risks for the country.

rule: In the context of Recommendation 1, “proliferation financing risk” refers strictly and only to the potential breach, non-implementation or evasion of the targeted financial sanctions obligations referred to in Recommendation 7.
nlc: Verify that in the context of Recommendation 1, the term “proliferation financing risk” refers strictly and only to the potential breach, non-implementation or evasion of the targeted financial sanctions obligations referred to in Recommendation 7.

rule: Countries should take proportionate action aimed at ensuring that these risks are mitigated effectively, including designating an authority or mechanism to coordinate actions to assess risks, and allocate resources efficiently for this purpose.
nlc: Verify that countries take proportionate action aimed at ensuring that risks are mitigated effectively, including designating an authority or mechanism to coordinate actions to assess risks and allocating resources efficiently for this purpose.

rule: Where countries identify higher risks, they should ensure that they adequately address such risks.
nlc: Verify that where countries identify higher risks, they adequately address such risks.

rule: Where countries identify lower risks, they should ensure that the measures applied are proportionate to the level of proliferation financing risk, while still ensuring full implementation of the targeted financial sanctions as required in Recommendation 7.
nlc: Verify that where countries identify lower risks, the measures applied are proportionate to the level of proliferation financing risk, while still ensuring full implementation of the targeted financial sanctions as required in Recommendation 7.

rule: Countries should require financial institutions and designated non-financial businesses and professions (DNFBPs) to identify, assess and take effective and risk-based action to mitigate their money laundering, terrorist financing and proliferation financing risks.
nlc: Verify that countries require financial institutions and designated non-financial businesses and professions (DNFBPs) to identify, assess and take effective and risk-based action to mitigate their money laundering, terrorist financing and proliferation financing risks.

rule: Countries should have national AML/CFT/CPF policies, informed by the risks1 identified, which should be regularly reviewed, and should designate an authority or have a coordination or other mechanism that is responsible for such policies.
nlc: Verify that countries have national AML/CFT/CPF policies that are informed by identified risks, are regularly reviewed, and that an authority has been designated or a coordination or other mechanism is in place which is responsible for such policies.

rule: Countries should ensure that policy-makers, the financial intelligence unit (FIU), law enforcement authorities, supervisors and other relevant competent authorities, at the policymaking and operational levels, have effective mechanisms in place which enable them to cooperate, and, where appropriate, coordinate and exchange information domestically with each other concerning the development and implementation of policies and activities to combat money laundering, terrorist financing and the financing of proliferation of weapons of mass destruction.
nlc: Verify that policy-makers, the financial intelligence unit (FIU), law enforcement authorities, supervisors and other relevant competent authorities, at the policy-making and operational levels, have effective mechanisms in place which enable them to cooperate, and, where appropriate, coordinate and exchange information domestically with each other concerning the development and implementation of policies and activities to combat money laundering, terrorist financing and the financing of proliferation of weapons of mass destruction.

rule: This should include cooperation and coordination between relevant authorities to ensure the compatibility of AML/CFT/CPF requirements with Data Protection and Privacy rules and other similar provisions (e.g. data security/localisation).
nlc: Verify that there is cooperation and coordination between relevant authorities to ensure the compatibility of AML/CFT/CPF requirements with Data Protection and Privacy rules and other similar provisions (e.g. data security/localisation).

rule: (Proliferation financing risk refers strictly and only to the potential breach, non-implementation or evasion of the targeted financial sanctions obligations referred to in Recommendation 7)
nlc: Verify that proliferation financing risk refers strictly and only to the potential breach, non-implementation or evasion of the targeted financial sanctions obligations referred to in Recommendation 7.

rule: Countries should criminalize money laundering on the basis of the Vienna Convention and the Palermo Convention.
nlc: Verify that countries criminalize money laundering on the basis of the Vienna Convention and the Palermo Convention.

rule: Countries should apply the crime of money laundering to all serious offences, with a view to including the widest range of predicate offences.
nlc: Verify that countries apply the crime of money laundering to all serious offences, with a view to including the widest range of predicate offences.

rule: Countries should ensure that they have policies and operational frameworks that prioritise asset recovery in both the domestic and international context.
nlc: Verify that countries have policies and operational frameworks that prioritise asset recovery in both the domestic and international context.

rule: Taking into account the Vienna Convention, the Palermo Convention, the United Nations Convention against Corruption, and the Terrorist Financing Convention, countries should have measures, including legislative measures, to enable their competent authorities to: a) identify, trace and evaluate criminal property and property of corresponding value; b) suspend or withhold consent to a transaction; c) take any appropriate investigative measures; d) expeditiously carry out provisional measures, such as freezing and seizing, to prevent any dealing, transfer or disposal of criminal property and property of corresponding value; e) confiscate criminal property and property of corresponding value through conviction-based confiscation; f) confiscate criminal property through non-conviction based confiscation; g) enforce a resulting confiscation order; and h) ensure effective management of property that is frozen, seized or confiscated.
nlc: Verify that, in accordance with the Vienna Convention, the Palermo Convention, the United Nations Convention against Corruption, and the Terrorist Financing Convention, the country has measures, including legislative measures, that enable its competent authorities to: a) identify, trace and evaluate criminal property and property of corresponding value; b) suspend or withhold consent to a transaction; c) take any appropriate investigative measures; d) expeditiously carry out provisional measures, such as freezing and seizing, to prevent any dealing, transfer or disposal of such property; e) confiscate criminal property and property of corresponding value through conviction-based confiscation; f) confiscate criminal property through non-conviction based confiscation; g) enforce a resulting confiscation order; and h) ensure effective management of property that is frozen, seized or confiscated.

rule: Countries should criminalise terrorist financing on the basis of the Terrorist Financing Convention, and should criminalise not only the financing of terrorist acts but also the financing of terrorist organisations and individual terrorists even in the absence of a link to a specific terrorist act or acts.
nlc: Verify that countries criminalise terrorist financing on the basis of the Terrorist Financing Convention, and criminalise not only the financing of terrorist acts but also the financing of terrorist organisations and individual terrorists even in the absence of a link to a specific terrorist act or acts.

rule: Countries should ensure that such offences are designated as money laundering predicate offences.
nlc: Verify that such offences are designated as money laundering predicate offences.

rule: Countries should implement targeted financial sanctions regimes to comply with United Nations Security Council resolutions relating to the prevention and suppression of terrorism and terrorist financing.
nlc: Verify that countries have implemented targeted financial sanctions regimes to comply with United Nations Security Council resolutions relating to the prevention and suppression of terrorism and terrorist financing.

rule: The resolutions require countries to freeze without delay the funds or other assets of, and to ensure that no funds or other assets are made available, directly or indirectly, to or for the benefit of, any person or entity either (i) designated by, or under the authority of, the United Nations Security Council under Chapter VII of the Charter of the United Nations, including in accordance with resolution 1267 (1999) and its successor resolutions; or (ii) designated by that country pursuant to resolution 1373 (2001).
nlc: Verify that countries freeze without delay the funds or other assets of, and ensure that no funds or other assets are made available, directly or indirectly, to or for the benefit of, any person or entity either (i) designated by, or under the authority of, the United Nations Security Council under Chapter VII of the Charter of the United Nations, including in accordance with resolution 1267 (1999) and its successor resolutions; or (ii) designated by that country pursuant to resolution 1373 (2001).

rule: Countries should implement targeted financial sanctions to comply with United Nations Security Council resolutions relating to the prevention, suppression and disruption of proliferation of weapons of mass destruction and its financing.
nlc: Verify that countries have implemented targeted financial sanctions to comply with United Nations Security Council resolutions relating to the prevention, suppression and disruption of proliferation of weapons of mass destruction and its financing.

rule: These resolutions require countries to freeze without delay the funds or other assets of, and to ensure that no funds and other assets are made available, directly or indirectly, to or for the benefit of, any person or entity designated by, or under the authority of, the United Nations Security Council under Chapter VII of the Charter of the United Nations.
nlc: Verify that the funds or other assets of any person or entity designated by the United Nations Security Council under Chapter VII of the Charter of the United Nations are frozen without delay, and that no funds or other assets are made available, directly or indirectly, to or for their benefit.

rule: Countries should identify the organisations which fall within the FATF definition of non-profit organisations (NPOs) and assess their terrorist financing risks.
nlc: Verify that countries have identified the organisations which fall within the FATF definition of non-profit organisations (NPOs) and have assessed their terrorist financing risks.

rule: Countries should have in place focused, proportionate and risk-based measures, without unduly disrupting or discouraging legitimate NPO activities, in line with the risk-based approach.
nlc: Verify that countries have in place focused, proportionate and risk-based measures, without unduly disrupting or discouraging legitimate NPO activities, in line with the risk-based approach.

rule: Countries should ensure that financial institution secrecy laws do not inhibit implementation of the FATF Recommendations.
nlc: Verify that countries are ensuring financial institution secrecy laws do not impede the implementation of the FATF Recommendations

rule: Financial institutions should be prohibited from keeping anonymous accounts or accounts in obviously fictitious names.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions do not keep anonymous accounts or accounts in obviously fictitious names.
sql: SELECT * FROM samld r1 WHERE r1.Sender_account IS NULL OR r1.Receiver_account IS NULL;

rule: Financial institutions should be required to undertake customer due diligence (CDD) measures when: (i) establishing business relations; (ii) carrying out occasional transactions: (i) above the applicable designated threshold (USD/EUR 15,000); or (ii) that are wire transfers in the circumstances covered by the Interpretive Note to Recommendation 16; (iii) there is a suspicion of money laundering or terrorist financing; or (iv) the financial institution has doubts about the veracity or adequacy of previously obtained customer identification data.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions undertake customer due diligence (CDD) measures when: (i) establishing business relations; (ii) carrying out occasional transactions above the applicable designated threshold of USD/EUR 15,000 or that are wire transfers under the circumstances covered by the Interpretive Note to Recommendation 16; (iii) there is a suspicion of money laundering or terrorist financing; or (iv) the financial institution has doubts about the veracity or adequacy of previously obtained customer identification data.
sql: SELECT * FROM samld r1 WHERE r1.Amount > 15000 AND r1.Payment_currency IN ('USD', 'EUR');

rule: The principle that financial institutions should conduct CDD should be set out in law.
nlc: Verify that the principle that financial institutions should conduct CDD is set out in law.

rule: Each country may determine how it imposes specific CDD obligations, either through law or enforceable means.
nlc: Check if each country determines how it imposes specific CDD obligations, either through law or enforceable means.

rule: The CDD measures to be taken are as follows: (a) Identifying the customer and verifying that customer’s identity using reliable, independent source documents, data or information. (b) Identifying the beneficial owner, and taking reasonable measures to verify the identity of the beneficial owner, such that the financial institution is satisfied that it knows who the beneficial owner is. For legal persons and arrangements this should include financial institutions understanding the ownership and control structure of the customer. (c) Understanding and, as appropriate, obtaining information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship. (d) Conducting ongoing due diligence on the business relationship and scrutiny of transactions undertaken throughout the course of that relationship to ensure that the transactions being conducted are consistent with the institution’s knowledge of the customer, their business and risk profile, including, where necessary, the source of funds.
nlc: Verify that: (a) the customer is identified and verified using reliable, independent source documents, data or information; (b) the beneficial owner is identified and verified, including understanding the ownership and control structure of legal persons and arrangements; (c) the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship is understood and documented; and (d) ongoing due diligence and transaction scrutiny are conducted to ensure consistency with the customer’s business, risk profile, and source of funds.

rule: Financial institutions should be required to apply each of the CDD measures under (a) to (d) above, but should determine the extent of such measures using a risk-based approach (RBA) in accordance with the Interpretive Notes to this Recommendation and to Recommendation 1.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions apply each of the CDD measures under (a) to (d) and determine the extent of such measures using a risk-based approach (RBA) in accordance with the Interpretive Notes to this Recommendation and to Recommendation 1.

rule: Financial institutions should be required to verify the identity of the customer and beneficial owner before or during the course of establishing a business relationship or conducting transactions for occasional customers.
nlc: Verify that for any business relationship or transaction for an occasional customer, the financial institution has verified the identity of the customer and beneficial owner before or during the establishment of that relationship or transaction.

rule: Countries may permit financial institutions to complete the verification as soon as reasonably practicable following the establishment of the relationship, where the money laundering and terrorist financing risks are effectively managed and where this is essential not to interrupt the normal conduct of business.
nlc: Verify that where financial institutions complete verification after the establishment of the relationship, it is done as soon as reasonably practicable, money laundering and terrorist financing risks are effectively managed, and it is essential for not interrupting the normal conduct of business.

rule: Where the financial institution is unable to comply with the applicable requirements under paragraphs (a) to (d) above (subject to appropriate modification of the extent of the measures on a risk-based approach), it should be required not to open the account, commence business relations or perform the transaction; or should be required to terminate the business relationship; and should consider making a suspicious transactions report in relation to the customer.
nlc: Check if, where the financial institution is unable to comply with the applicable requirements under paragraphs (a) to (d), it is required not to open the account, commence business relations, or perform the transaction; or is required to terminate the business relationship; and considers making a suspicious transactions report in relation to the customer.

rule: These requirements should apply to all new customers, although financial institutions should also apply this Recommendation to existing customers on the basis of materiality and risk, and should conduct due diligence on such existing relationships at appropriate times.
nlc: Verify that these requirements are applied to all new customers, and that for existing customers, this Recommendation is also applied on the basis of materiality and risk, and that due diligence is conducted on such existing relationships at appropriate times.

rule: Financial institutions should be required to maintain, for at least five years, all necessary records on transactions, both domestic and international, to enable them to comply swiftly with information requests from the competent authorities.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions maintain, for at least five years, all necessary records on transactions, both domestic and international, to enable them to comply swiftly with information requests from the competent authorities.
dc: t("SELECT MAX(samld.Date) - MIN(samld.Date) AS dif_data FROM samld")["t.dif_data < 5"]
sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT MAX(samld.Date) - MIN(samld.Date) AS dif_data FROM samld) t WHERE t.dif_data < 5;

rule: Such records must be sufficient to permit reconstruction of individual transactions (including the amounts and types of currency involved, if any) so as to provide, if necessary, evidence for prosecution of criminal activity.
nlc: Verify that records are sufficient to permit reconstruction of individual transactions, including the amounts and types of currency involved, so as to provide, if necessary, evidence for prosecution of criminal activity.
sql: SELECT * FROM samld WHERE Amount <= 0 OR Payment_currency IS NULL OR TRIM(Payment_currency) = '' OR Received_currency IS NULL OR TRIM(Received_currency) = '';

rule: Financial institutions should be required to keep all records obtained through CDD measures (e.g. copies or records of official identification documents like passports, identity cards, driving licences or similar documents), account files and business correspondence, including the results of any analysis undertaken (e.g. inquiries to establish the background and purpose of complex, unusual large transactions), for at least five years after the business relationship is ended, or after the date of the occasional transaction.
nlc: Verify that for any financial institution, all records obtained through CDD measures (including copies or records of official identification documents like passports, identity cards, or driving licences), account files, and business correspondence, including the results of any analysis undertaken, are kept for at least five years after the business relationship has ended or after the date of an occasional transaction.

rule: Financial institutions should be required by law to maintain records on transactions and information obtained through the CDD measures.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions maintain records on transactions and information obtained through the CDD measures.
sql: SELECT * FROM samld r1 WHERE r1.Is_Laundering IS NULL OR r1.Laundering_type IS NULL;

rule: The CDD information and the transaction records should be available to domestic competent authorities upon appropriate authority.
nlc: Verify that the CDD information and the transaction records are available to domestic competent authorities upon appropriate authority.

rule: Financial institutions should be required, in relation to foreign politically exposed persons (PEPs) (whether as customer or beneficial owner), in addition to performing normal customer due diligence measures, to: (a) have appropriate risk-management systems to determine whether the customer or the beneficial owner is a politically exposed person; (b) obtain senior management approval for establishing (or continuing, for existing customers) such business relationships; (c) take reasonable measures to establish the source of wealth and source of funds; and (d) conduct enhanced ongoing monitoring of the business relationship.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions, in relation to foreign politically exposed persons (PEPs) as either customer or beneficial owner, in addition to performing normal customer due diligence measures, have: (a) appropriate risk-management systems to determine whether the customer or beneficial owner is a politically exposed person; (b) obtained senior management approval for establishing or continuing such business relationships; (c) taken reasonable measures to establish the source of wealth and source of funds; and (d) conducted enhanced ongoing monitoring of the business relationship.

rule: Financial institutions should be required to take reasonable measures to determine whether a customer or beneficial owner is a domestic PEP or a person who is or has been entrusted with a prominent function by an international organisation.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions take reasonable measures to determine whether a customer or beneficial owner is a domestic PEP or a person who is or has been entrusted with a prominent function by an international organisation.

rule: In cases of a higher risk business relationship with such persons, financial institutions should be required to apply the measures referred to in paragraphs (b), (c) and (d).
nlc: Verify that in cases of a higher risk business relationship with such persons, financial institutions apply the measures referred to in paragraphs (b), (c) and (d).

rule: The requirements for all types of PEP should also apply to family members or close associates of such PEPs.
nlc: Verify that the requirements for all types of PEP also apply to family members or close associates of such PEPs.

rule: Financial institutions should be required, in relation to cross-border correspondent banking and other similar relationships, in addition to performing normal customer due diligence measures, to: (a) gather sufficient information about a respondent institution to understand fully the nature of the respondent’s business and to determine from publicly available information the reputation of the institution and the quality of supervision, including whether it has been subject to a money laundering or terrorist financing investigation or regulatory action; (b) assess the respondent institution’s AML/CFT controls; (c) obtain approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships; (d) clearly understand the respective responsibilities of each institution; and (e) with respect to “payable-through accounts”, be satisfied that the respondent bank has conducted CDD on the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent bank, and that it is able to provide relevant CDD information upon request to the correspondent bank.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions, in relation to cross-border correspondent banking and other similar relationships, in addition to performing normal customer due diligence measures, have: (a) gathered sufficient information about a respondent institution to understand fully the nature of the respondent’s business and to determine from publicly available information the reputation of the institution and the quality of supervision, including whether it has been subject to a money laundering or terrorist financing investigation or regulatory action; (b) assessed the respondent institution’s AML/CFT controls; (c) obtained approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships; (d) clearly understood the respective responsibilities of each institution; and (e) with respect to “payable-through accounts”, satisfied themselves that the respondent bank has conducted CDD on the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent bank, and that it is able to provide relevant CDD information upon request to the correspondent bank.

rule: Financial institutions should be prohibited from entering into, or continuing, a correspondent banking relationship with shell banks.
nlc: Verify that no financial institution has entered into or is continuing a correspondent banking relationship with a shell bank.

rule: Financial institutions should be required to satisfy themselves that respondent institutions do not permit their accounts to be used by shell banks.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions have confirmed that their respondent institutions do not permit their accounts to be used by shell banks.

rule: Countries should take measures to ensure that natural or legal persons that provide money or value transfer services (MVTS) are licensed or registered, and subject to effective systems for monitoring and ensuring compliance with the relevant measures called for in the FATF Recommendations.
nlc: Verify that natural or legal persons that provide money or value transfer services (MVTS) are licensed or registered, and subject to effective systems for monitoring and ensuring compliance with the relevant measures called for in the FATF Recommendations.

rule: Countries should take action to identify natural or legal persons that carry out MVTS without a license or registration, and to apply appropriate sanctions.
nlc: Verify that countries take action to identify natural or legal persons that carry out MVTS without a license or registration, and apply appropriate sanctions.

rule: Any natural or legal person working as an agent should also be licensed or registered by a competent authority, or the MVTS provider should maintain a current list of its agents accessible by competent authorities in the countries in which the MVTS provider and its agents operate.
nlc: Verify that any natural or legal person working as an agent is licensed or registered by a competent authority, or the MVTS provider maintains a current list of its agents accessible by competent authorities in the countries in which the MVTS provider and its agents operate.

rule: Countries should take measures to ensure that MVTS providers that use agents include them in their AML/CFT programmes and monitor them for compliance with these programmes.
nlc: Verify that countries have taken measures to ensure that MVTS providers that use agents include them in their AML/CFT programmes and monitor them for compliance with these programmes.

rule: Countries and financial institutions should identify and assess the money laundering or terrorist financing risks that may arise in relation to (a) the development of new products and new business practices, including new delivery mechanisms, and (b) the use of new or developing technologies for both new and pre-existing products.
nlc: Verify that countries and financial institutions identify and assess the money laundering or terrorist financing risks that may arise in relation to the development of new products and new business practices, including new delivery mechanisms, and the use of new or developing technologies for both new and pre-existing products.

rule: In the case of financial institutions, such a risk assessment should take place prior to the launch of the new products, business practices or the use of new or developing technologies.
nlc: Verify that for financial institutions, a risk assessment takes place prior to the launch of new products, business practices, or the use of new or developing technologies.

rule: They should take appropriate measures to manage and mitigate those risks.
nlc: Verify that appropriate measures have been taken to manage and mitigate those risks.

rule: To manage and mitigate the risks emerging from virtual assets, countries should ensure that virtual asset service providers are regulated for AML/CFT purposes, and licensed or registered and subject to effective systems for monitoring and ensuring compliance with the relevant measures called for in the FATF Recommendations.
nlc: Verify that virtual asset service providers are regulated for AML/CFT purposes, are licensed or registered, and are subject to effective systems for monitoring and ensuring compliance with the relevant measures called for in the FATF Recommendations.

rule: Countries should ensure that financial institutions include required and accurate originator information, and required beneficiary information, on wire transfers and related messages, and that the information remains with the wire transfer or related message throughout the payment chain.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions include required and accurate originator information, and required beneficiary information, on wire transfers and related messages, and that the information remains with the wire transfer or related message throughout the payment chain.
dc: ["r1.Payment_type LIKE '%Wire%'", "r1.Sender_account IS NULL"], ["r1.Payment_type LIKE '%Wire%'", "r1.Receiver_account IS NULL)"]
sql: SELECT * FROM samld r1 WHERE r1.Payment_type LIKE '%Wire%' AND (r1.Sender_account IS NULL OR r1.Receiver_account IS NULL);

rule: Countries should ensure that financial institutions monitor wire transfers for the purpose of detecting those which lack required originator and/or beneficiary information, and take appropriate measures.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions monitor wire transfers to detect transfers that lack the required originator and/or beneficiary information, and that appropriate measures are taken.
dc: ["r1.Payment_type LIKE '%Wire%'", "r1.Sender_account IS NULL"], ["r1.Payment_type LIKE '%Wire%'", "r1.Receiver_account IS NULL)"]
sql: SELECT * FROM samld r1 WHERE r1.Payment_type LIKE '%Wire%' AND (r1.Sender_account IS NULL OR r1.Receiver_account IS NULL);

rule: Countries should ensure that, in the context of processing wire transfers, financial institutions take freezing action and should prohibit conducting transactions with designated persons and entities, as per the obligations set out in the relevant United Nations Security Council resolutions, such as resolution 1267 (1999) and its successor resolutions, and resolution 1373(2001), relating to the prevention and suppression of terrorism and terrorist financing.
nlc: Verify that, in the context of processing wire transfers, financial institutions take freezing action and prohibit conducting transactions with designated persons and entities, as per the obligations set out in the relevant United Nations Security Council resolutions, such as resolution 1267 (1999) and its successor resolutions, and resolution 1373(2001), relating to the prevention and suppression of terrorism and terrorist financing.

rule: Countries may permit financial institutions to rely on third parties to perform elements (a)-(c) of the CDD measures set out in Recommendation 10 or to introduce business, provided that the criteria set out below are met.
nlc: Verify that where countries permit financial institutions to rely on third parties to perform elements (a)-(c) of the CDD measures set out in Recommendation 10 or to introduce business, the required criteria are met.

rule: Where such reliance is permitted, the ultimate responsibility for CDD measures remains with the financial institution relying on the third party.
nlc: Verify that where reliance on a third party for CDD measures is permitted, the ultimate responsibility for those measures remains with the financial institution relying on the third party.

rule: A financial institution relying upon a third party should immediately obtain the necessary information concerning elements (a)-(c) of the CDD measures set out in Recommendation 10.
nlc: Verify that a financial institution relying upon a third party immediately obtains the necessary information concerning elements (a)-(c) of the CDD measures set out in Recommendation 10.

rule: Financial institutions should take adequate steps to satisfy themselves that copies of identification data and other relevant documentation relating to the CDD requirements will be made available from the third party upon request without delay.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions have taken adequate steps to ensure that copies of identification data and other relevant documentation relating to CDD requirements will be made available from the third party upon request without delay.

rule: The financial institution should satisfy itself that the third party is regulated, supervised or monitored for, and has measures in place for compliance with, CDD and record- keeping requirements in line with Recommendations 10 and 11.
nlc: Verify that the third party is regulated, supervised or monitored for, and has measures in place for compliance with, CDD and record-keeping requirements in line with Recommendations 10 and 11.

rule: When determining in which countries the third party that meets the conditions can be based, countries should have regard to information available on the level of country risk.
nlc: Verify that when determining in which countries a third party can be based, regard is given to available information on the level of country risk.

rule: When a financial institution relies on a third party that is part of the same financial group, and (i) that group applies CDD and record-keeping requirements, in line with Recommendations 10, 11 and 12, and programmes against money laundering and terrorist financing, in accordance with Recommendation 18; and (ii) where the effective implementation of those CDD and record-keeping requirements and AML/CFT programmes is supervised at a group level by a competent authority, then relevant competent authorities may consider that the financial institution applies measures under (b) and (c) above through its group programme, and may decide that (d) is not a necessary precondition to reliance when higher country risk is adequately mitigated by the group AML/CFT policies.
nlc: Verify that when a financial institution relies on a third party from the same financial group and is permitted to do so without meeting precondition (d) because higher country risk is mitigated by group policies, that both of the following conditions are met: (i) the group applies CDD and record-keeping requirements in line with Recommendations 10, 11, and 12, and AML/CFT programmes in accordance with Recommendation 18; and (ii) the effective implementation of these requirements and programmes is supervised at a group level by a competent authority.

rule: Financial institutions should be required to implement programmes against money laundering and terrorist financing.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions have implemented programmes against money laundering and terrorist financing.

rule: Financial groups should be required to implement group-wide programmes against money laundering and terrorist financing, including policies and procedures for sharing information within the group for AML/CFT purposes.
nlc: Verify that financial groups have implemented group-wide programmes against money laundering and terrorist financing, including policies and procedures for sharing information within the group for AML/CFT purposes.

rule: Financial institutions should be required to ensure that their foreign branches and majority-owned subsidiaries apply AML/CFT measures consistent with the home country requirements implementing the FATF Recommendations through the financial groups’ programmes against money laundering and terrorist financing.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions ensure that their foreign branches and majority-owned subsidiaries apply AML/CFT measures consistent with the home country requirements implementing the FATF Recommendations through the financial groups’ programmes against money laundering and terrorist financing.

rule: Financial institutions should be required to apply enhanced due diligence measures to business relationships and transactions with natural and legal persons, and financial institutions, from countries for which this is called for by the FATF.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions apply enhanced due diligence measures to business relationships and transactions with natural and legal persons, and financial institutions, from countries for which this is called for by the FATF.

rule: The type of enhanced due diligence measures applied should be effective and proportionate to the risks.
nlc: Verify that the type of enhanced due diligence measures applied are effective and proportionate to the risks.

rule: Countries should be able to apply appropriate countermeasures when called upon to do so by the FATF.
nlc: Verify that countries are able to apply appropriate countermeasures when called upon to do so by the FATF.

rule: Countries should also be able to apply countermeasures independently of any call by the FATF to do so.
nlc: Verify that countries are able to apply countermeasures independently of any call by the FATF to do so.

rule: Such countermeasures should be effective and proportionate to the risks.
nlc: Verify that countermeasures are effective and proportionate to the risks.

rule: If a financial institution suspects or has reasonable grounds to suspect that funds are the proceeds of a criminal activity, or are related to terrorist financing, it should be required, by law, to report promptly its suspicions to the financial intelligence unit (FIU).
nlc: Verify that if a financial institution suspects or has reasonable grounds to suspect that funds are the proceeds of a criminal activity, or are related to terrorist financing, it promptly reports its suspicions to the financial intelligence unit (FIU).

rule: Financial institutions, their directors, officers and employees should be: (a) protected by law from criminal and civil liability for breach of any restriction on disclosure of information imposed by contract or by any legislative, regulatory or administrative provision, if they report their suspicions in good faith to the FIU, even if they did not know precisely what the underlying criminal activity was, and regardless of whether illegal activity actually occurred; and (b) prohibited by law from disclosing (“tipping-off”) the fact that a suspicious transaction report (STR) or related information is being filed with the FIU.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions, their directors, officers and employees are: (a) protected by law from criminal and civil liability for breach of any restriction on disclosure of information imposed by contract or by any legislative, regulatory or administrative provision, if they report their suspicions in good faith to the FIU, even if they did not know precisely what the underlying criminal activity was, and regardless of whether illegal activity actually occurred; and (b) prohibited by law from disclosing (“tipping-off”) the fact that a suspicious transaction report (STR) or related information is being filed with the FIU.

rule: The customer due diligence and record-keeping requirements set out in Recommendations 10, 11, 12, 15, and 17, apply to designated non-financial businesses and professions (DNFBPs) in the following situations: (a) Casinos  when customers engage in financial transactions equal to or above the applicable designated threshold. (b) Real estate agents  when they are involved in transactions for their client concerning the buying and selling of real estate. (c) Dealers in precious metals and dealers in precious stones  when they engage in any cash transaction with a customer equal to or above the applicable designated threshold. (d) Lawyers, notaries, other independent legal professionals and accountants  when they prepare for or carry out transactions for their client concerning the following activities:  buying and selling of real estate; managing of client money, securities or other assets; management of bank, savings or securities accounts; organisation of contributions for the creation, operation or management of companies; creation, operation or management of legal persons or arrangements, and buying and selling of business entities. e) Trust and company service providers  when they prepare for or carry out transactions for a client concerning the following activities: acting as a formation agent of legal persons;  acting as (or arranging for another person to act as) a director or secretary of a company, a partner of a partnership, or a similar position in relation to other legal persons;  providing a registered office, business address or accommodation, correspondence or administrative address for a company, a partnership or any other legal person or arrangement;  acting as (or arranging for another person to act as) a trustee of an express trust or performing the equivalent function for another form of legal arrangement;  acting as (or arranging for another person to act as) a nominee shareholder for another person.
nlc: Verify that the customer due diligence and record-keeping requirements set out in Recommendations 10, 11, 12, 15, and 17, are applied to designated non-financial businesses and professions (DNFBPs) in the following situations: (a) Casinos when customers engage in financial transactions equal to or above the applicable designated threshold. (b) Real estate agents when they are involved in transactions for their client concerning the buying and selling of real estate. (c) Dealers in precious metals and dealers in precious stones when they engage in any cash transaction with a customer equal to or above the applicable designated threshold. (d) Lawyers, notaries, other independent legal professionals and accountants when they prepare for or carry out transactions for their client concerning the following activities: buying and selling of real estate; managing of client money, securities or other assets; management of bank, savings or securities accounts; organisation of contributions for the creation, operation or management of companies; creation, operation or management of legal persons or arrangements, and buying and selling of business entities. (e) Trust and company service providers when they prepare for or carry out transactions for a client concerning the following activities: acting as a formation agent of legal persons; acting as (or arranging for another person to act as) a director or secretary of a company, a partner of a partnership, or a similar position in relation to other legal persons; providing a registered office, business address or accommodation, correspondence or administrative address for a company, a partnership or any other legal person or arrangement; acting as (or arranging for another person to act as) a trustee of an express trust or performing the equivalent function for another form of legal arrangement; acting as (or arranging for another person to act as) a nominee shareholder for another person.

rule: The requirements set out in Recommendations 18 to 21 apply to all designated non-financial businesses and professions, subject to the following qualifications:
nlc: Verify that the requirements set out in Recommendations 18 to 21 apply to all designated non-financial businesses and professions, subject to the specified qualifications.

rule: Lawyers, notaries, other independent legal professionals and accountants should be required to report suspicious transactions when, on behalf of or for a client, they engage in a financial transaction in relation to the activities described in paragraph (d) of Recommendation 22.
nlc: Verify that lawyers, notaries, other independent legal professionals and accountants report suspicious transactions when, on behalf of or for a client, they engage in a financial transaction in relation to the activities described in paragraph (d) of Recommendation 22.

rule: Countries are strongly encouraged to extend the reporting requirement to the rest of the professional activities of accountants, including auditing.
nlc: Check if countries have extended the reporting requirement to the rest of the professional activities of accountants, including auditing.

rule: Dealers in precious metals and dealers in precious stones should be required to report suspicious transactions when they engage in any cash transaction with a customer equal to or above the applicable designated threshold.
nlc: Verify that dealers in precious metals and dealers in precious stones report suspicious transactions when they engage in any cash transaction with a customer equal to or above the applicable designated threshold.

rule: Trust and company service providers should be required to report suspicious transactions for a client when, on behalf of or for a client, they engage in a transaction in relation to the activities referred to in paragraph (e) of Recommendation 22.
nlc: Verify that trust and company service providers report suspicious transactions for a client when, on behalf of or for a client, they engage in a transaction in relation to the activities referred to in paragraph (e) of Recommendation 22.

rule: Countries should assess the risks of the misuse of legal arrangements for money laundering or terrorist financing and take measures to prevent their misuse.
nlc: Verify that countries assess the risks of the misuse of legal arrangements for money laundering or terrorist financing and take measures to prevent their misuse.

rule: In particular, countries should ensure that there is adequate, accurate and up-to-date information on express trusts and other similar legal arrangements including information on the settlor(s), trustee(s) and beneficiary(ies), that can be obtained or accessed efficiently and in a timely manner by competent authorities, through either a register of beneficial ownership or an alternative mechanism.
nlc: Verify that for express trusts and other similar legal arrangements, there is adequate, accurate, and up-to-date information, including details on the settlor(s), trustee(s), and beneficiary(ies), which can be obtained or accessed efficiently and in a timely manner by competent authorities through a register of beneficial ownership or an alternative mechanism.

rule: Countries should not permit legal persons to issue new bearer shares or bearer share warrants, and take measures to prevent the misuse of existing bearer shares and bearer share warrants.
nlc: Verify that countries do not permit legal persons to issue new bearer shares or bearer share warrants, and that measures are taken to prevent the misuse of existing bearer shares and bearer share warrants.

rule: Countries should take effective measures to ensure that nominee shareholders and directors are not misused for money laundering or terrorist financing.
nlc: Verify that countries take effective measures to ensure that nominee shareholders and directors are not misused for money laundering or terrorist financing.

rule: Countries should consider facilitating access to beneficial ownership and control information by financial institutions and DNFBPs undertaking the requirements set out in Recommendations 10 and 22.
nlc: Verify that countries facilitate access to beneficial ownership and control information by financial institutions and DNFBPs undertaking the requirements set out in Recommendations 10 and 22.

rule: Countries should assess the risks of the misuse of legal arrangements for money laundering or terrorist financing and take measures to prevent their misuse.
nlc: Verify that countries assess the risks of the misuse of legal arrangements for money laundering or terrorist financing and that they take measures to prevent their misuse.

rule: In particular, countries should ensure that there is adequate, accurate and up-to-date information on express trusts and other similar legal arrangements including information on the settlor(s), trustee(s) and beneficiary(ies), that can be obtained or accessed efficiently and in a timely manner by competent authorities.
nlc: Verify that there is adequate, accurate and up-to-date information on express trusts and other similar legal arrangements, including information on the settlor(s), trustee(s) and beneficiary(ies), that can be obtained or accessed efficiently and in a timely manner by competent authorities.

rule: Countries should consider facilitating access to beneficial ownership and control information by financial institutions and DNFBPs undertaking the requirements set out in Recommendations 10 and 22.
nlc: Verify that countries facilitate access to beneficial ownership and control information for financial institutions and DNFBPs that are undertaking the requirements set out in Recommendations 10 and 22.

rule: Countries should ensure that financial institutions are subject to adequate regulation and supervision and are effectively implementing the FATF Recommendations.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions are subject to adequate regulation and supervision and are effectively implementing the FATF Recommendations.

rule: Competent authorities or financial supervisors should take the necessary legal or regulatory measures to prevent criminals or their associates from holding, or being the beneficial owner of, a significant or controlling interest, or holding a management function in, a financial institution.
nlc: Verify that no criminal or their associate holds, is the beneficial owner of, a significant or controlling interest, or holds a management function in a financial institution.

rule: Countries should not approve the establishment, or continued operation, of shell banks.
nlc: Verify that no country approves the establishment, or continued operation, of shell banks.

rule: For financial institutions subject to the Core Principles, the regulatory and supervisory measures that apply for prudential purposes, and which are also relevant to money laundering and terrorist financing, should apply in a similar manner for AML/CFT purposes.
nlc: Verify that for financial institutions subject to the Core Principles, the regulatory and supervisory measures applied for prudential purposes, which are also relevant to money laundering and terrorist financing, apply in a similar manner for AML/CFT purposes.

rule: This should include applying consolidated group supervision for AML/CFT purposes.
nlc: Verify that consolidated group supervision is applied for AML/CFT purposes.

rule: Other financial institutions should be licensed or registered and adequately regulated, and subject to supervision or monitoring for AML/CFT purposes, having regard to the risk of money laundering or terrorist financing in that sector.
nlc: Verify that other financial institutions are licensed or registered and adequately regulated, and subject to supervision or monitoring for AML/CFT purposes, having regard to the risk of money laundering or terrorist financing in that sector.

rule: At a minimum, where financial institutions provide a service of money or value transfer, or of money or currency changing, they should be licensed or registered, and subject to effective systems for monitoring and ensuring compliance with national AML/CFT requirements.
nlc: Verify that financial institutions that provide a service of money or value transfer, or of money or currency changing, are licensed or registered and subject to effective systems for monitoring and ensuring compliance with national AML/CFT requirements.

rule: Supervisors should have adequate powers to supervise or monitor, and ensure compliance by, financial institutions with requirements to combat money laundering and terrorist financing, including the authority to conduct inspections.
nlc: Verify that supervisors have adequate powers to supervise or monitor, and ensure compliance by, financial institutions with requirements to combat money laundering and terrorist financing, including the authority to conduct inspections.

rule: They should be authorised to compel production of any information from financial institutions that is relevant to monitoring such compliance, and to impose sanctions, in line with Recommendation 35, for failure to comply with such requirements.
nlc: Verify that they are authorised to compel production of any information from financial institutions that is relevant to monitoring compliance and to impose sanctions, in line with Recommendation 35, for failure to comply with such requirements.

rule: Supervisors should have powers to impose a range of disciplinary and financial sanctions, including the power to withdraw, restrict or suspend the financial institution’s license, where applicable.
nlc: Verify that supervisors have powers to impose a range of disciplinary and financial sanctions, including the power to withdraw, restrict or suspend the financial institution’s license, where applicable.

rule: Designated non-financial businesses and professions should be subject to regulatory and supervisory measures as set out below.
nlc: Verify that designated non-financial businesses and professions are subject to regulatory and supervisory measures as set out below.

rule: Casinos should be subject to a comprehensive regulatory and supervisory regime that ensures that they have effectively implemented the necessary AML/CFT measures.
nlc: Verify that casinos are subject to a comprehensive regulatory and supervisory regime that ensures that they have effectively implemented the necessary AML/CFT measures.

rule: At a minimum: casinos should be licensed;
nlc: Verify that casinos are licensed.

rule: competent authorities should take the necessary legal or regulatory measures to prevent criminals or their associates from holding, or being the beneficial owner of, a significant or controlling interest, holding a management function in, or being an operator of, a casino;
nlc: Verify that no criminal or their associate holds, or is the beneficial owner of, a significant or controlling interest, holds a management function in, or is an operator of, a casino.

rule: competent authorities should ensure that casinos are effectively supervised for compliance with AML/CFT requirements.
nlc: Verify that competent authorities ensure that casinos are effectively supervised for compliance with AML/CFT requirements

rule: Countries should ensure that the other categories of DNFBPs are subject to effective systems for monitoring and ensuring compliance with AML/CFT requirements.
nlc: Verify that other categories of DNFBPs are subject to effective systems for monitoring and ensuring compliance with AML/CFT requirements.

rule: This should be performed on a risk-sensitive basis.
nlc: Verify that this is performed on a risk-sensitive basis.

rule: This may be performed by (a) a supervisor or (b) by an appropriate self-regulatory body (SRB), provided that such a body can ensure that its members comply with their obligations to combat money laundering and terrorist financing.
nlc: Verify that the performance is by either a supervisor or an appropriate self-regulatory body (SRB), and that if it is by an SRB, that body can ensure its members comply with their obligations to combat money laundering and terrorist financing.

rule: The supervisor or SRB should also (a) take the necessary measures to prevent criminals or their associates from being professionally accredited, or holding or being the beneficial owner of a significant or controlling interest or holding a management function, e.g. through evaluating persons on the basis of a “fit and proper” test; and (b) have effective, proportionate, and dissuasive sanctions in line with Recommendation 35 available to deal with failure to comply with AML/CFT requirements.
nlc: Verify that the supervisor or SRB (a) takes the necessary measures, such as a “fit and proper” test, to prevent criminals or their associates from being professionally accredited, holding or being the beneficial owner of a significant or controlling interest, or holding a management function; and (b) has effective, proportionate, and dissuasive sanctions in line with Recommendation 35 available to deal with failures to comply with AML/CFT requirements.

rule: Countries should establish a financial intelligence unit (FIU) that serves as a national centre for the receipt and analysis of: (a) suspicious transaction reports; and (b) other information relevant to money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing, and for the dissemination of the results of that analysis.
nlc: Verify that each country has established a financial intelligence unit (FIU) that serves as a national centre for the receipt and analysis of: (a) suspicious transaction reports; and (b) other information relevant to money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing, and for the dissemination of the results of that analysis.

rule: The FIU should be able to obtain additional information from reporting entities, and should have access on a timely basis to the financial, administrative and law enforcement information that it requires to undertake its functions properly.
nlc: Verify that the FIU is able to obtain additional information from reporting entities, and has access on a timely basis to the financial, administrative and law enforcement information that it requires to undertake its functions properly.

rule: Countries should ensure that designated law enforcement authorities have responsibility for money laundering and terrorist financing investigations within the framework of national AML/CFT policies.
nlc: Verify that designated law enforcement authorities have responsibility for money laundering and terrorist financing investigations within the framework of national AML/CFT policies.

rule: At least in all cases related to major proceeds-generating offences, these designated law enforcement authorities should develop a pro-active parallel financial investigation when pursuing money laundering, predicate offences and terrorist financing.
nlc: Verify that in all cases related to major proceeds-generating offences, designated law enforcement authorities develop a pro-active parallel financial investigation when pursuing money laundering, predicate offences and terrorist financing.

rule: This should include cases where the associated predicate offence occurs outside their jurisdictions.
nlc: Verify that cases where the associated predicate offence occurs outside their jurisdictions are included.

rule: Countries should ensure that competent authorities have responsibility for expeditiously identifying, tracing and initiating actions to freeze and seize criminal property and property of corresponding value.
nlc: Verify that competent authorities have responsibility for expeditiously identifying, tracing and initiating actions to freeze and seize criminal property and property of corresponding value.

rule: Countries should also make use, when necessary, of permanent or temporary multi-disciplinary groups specialised in financial or asset investigations.
nlc: Verify that countries make use, when necessary, of permanent or temporary multi-disciplinary groups specialised in financial or asset investigations.

rule: Countries should ensure that, when necessary, cooperative investigations with appropriate competent authorities in other countries take place.
nlc: Verify that, when necessary, cooperative investigations with appropriate competent authorities in other countries take place.

rule: When conducting investigations of money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing, competent authorities should be able to obtain access to all necessary documents and information for use in those investigations, and in prosecutions and related actions.
nlc: Verify that when conducting investigations of money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing, competent authorities are able to obtain access to all necessary documents and information for use in those investigations, and in prosecutions and related actions.

rule: This should include powers to use compulsory measures for the production of records held by financial institutions, DNFBPs and other natural or legal persons, for the search of persons and premises, for taking witness statements, and for the seizure and obtaining of evidence.
nlc: Verify that there are powers to use compulsory measures for the production of records held by financial institutions, DNFBPs and other natural or legal persons, for the search of persons and premises, for taking witness statements, and for the seizure and obtaining of evidence.

rule: Countries should ensure that competent authorities conducting investigations are able to use a wide range of investigative techniques suitable for the investigation of money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing.
nlc: Verify that competent authorities conducting investigations are able to use a wide range of investigative techniques suitable for the investigation of money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing.

rule: These investigative techniques include: undercover operations, intercepting communications, accessing computer systems and controlled delivery.
nlc: Verify that investigative techniques include only the following: undercover operations, intercepting communications, accessing computer systems, and controlled delivery.

rule: Countries should ensure that competent authorities have timely access to a wide range of information, particularly to support the identification and tracing of criminal property and property of corresponding value.
nlc: Verify that competent authorities have timely access to a wide range of information to support the identification and tracing of criminal property and property of corresponding value.

rule: This may include, but is not limited to, basic and beneficial ownership information, information held by tax authorities, information held in asset registries (such as for land, property, vehicles, shares, or other assets), and information held in citizenship, residency, or social benefit registries.
nlc: Verify that the information includes, but is not limited to, basic and beneficial ownership information, information held by tax authorities, information held in asset registries (such as for land, property, vehicles, shares, or other assets), and information held in citizenship, residency, or social benefit registries.

rule: In addition, countries should have effective mechanisms in place to identify, in a timely manner, whether natural or legal persons hold or control accounts.
nlc: Verify that countries have effective mechanisms in place to identify, in a timely manner, whether natural or legal persons hold or control accounts.

rule: They should also have mechanisms to ensure that competent authorities have a process to identify assets without prior notification to the owner.
nlc: Verify that a process is in place for competent authorities to identify assets without prior notification to the owner.

rule: When conducting investigations of money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing, competent authorities should be able to ask for all relevant information held by the FIU.
nlc: Verify that when conducting investigations of money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing, competent authorities are able to ask for all relevant information held by the FIU.

rule: Countries should have measures in place to detect the physical cross-border transportation of currency and bearer negotiable instruments, including through a declaration system and/or disclosure system.
nlc: Verify that countries have measures in place to detect the physical cross-border transportation of currency and bearer negotiable instruments, including through a declaration system and/or disclosure system.

rule: Countries should ensure that their competent authorities have the legal authority to stop or restrain currency or bearer negotiable instruments that are suspected to be related to terrorist financing, money laundering or predicate offences, or that are falsely declared or disclosed.
nlc: Verify that competent authorities have the legal authority to stop or restrain currency or bearer negotiable instruments that are suspected to be related to terrorist financing, money laundering or predicate offences, or that are falsely declared or disclosed.

rule: Countries should ensure that effective, proportionate and dissuasive sanctions are available to deal with persons who make false declaration(s) or disclosure(s).
nlc: Verify that effective, proportionate and dissuasive sanctions are available to deal with persons who make false declaration(s) or disclosure(s).

rule: In cases where the currency or bearer negotiable instruments are related to terrorist financing, money laundering or predicate offences, countries should also adopt measures, including legislative ones consistent with Recommendation 4, which would enable the confiscation of such currency or instruments.
nlc: Verify that in cases where currency or bearer negotiable instruments are related to terrorist financing, money laundering or predicate offences, countries have adopted measures, including legislative ones consistent with Recommendation 4, which enable the confiscation of such currency or instruments.

rule: Countries should maintain comprehensive statistics on matters relevant to the effectiveness and efficiency of their AML/CFT systems.
nlc: Verify that countries maintain comprehensive statistics on matters relevant to the effectiveness and efficiency of their AML/CFT systems.

rule: This should include statistics on the STRs received and disseminated; on money laundering and terrorist financing investigations, prosecutions and convictions; on property frozen, seized and confiscated; and on mutual legal assistance or other international requests for cooperation.
nlc: Verify that statistics are included on the STRs received and disseminated; on money laundering and terrorist financing investigations, prosecutions and convictions; on property frozen, seized and confiscated; and on mutual legal assistance or other international requests for cooperation.

rule: The competent authorities, supervisors and SRBs should establish guidelines, and provide feedback, which will assist financial institutions and designated non-financial businesses and professions in applying national measures to combat money laundering and terrorist financing, and, in particular, in detecting and reporting suspicious transactions.
nlc: Verify that the competent authorities, supervisors and SRBs establish guidelines, and provide feedback, which will assist financial institutions and designated non-financial businesses and professions in applying national measures to combat money laundering and terrorist financing, and, in particular, in detecting and reporting suspicious transactions.

rule: Countries should ensure that there is a range of effective, proportionate and dissuasive sanctions, whether criminal, civil or administrative, available to deal with natural or legal persons covered by Recommendations 6, and 8 to 23, that fail to comply with AML/CFT requirements.
nlc: Verify that there is a range of effective, proportionate and dissuasive sanctions, whether criminal, civil or administrative, available to deal with natural or legal persons covered by Recommendations 6, and 8 to 23, that fail to comply with AML/CFT requirements.

rule: Sanctions should be applicable not only to financial institutions and DNFBPs, but also to their directors and senior management.
nlc: Verify that sanctions are applicable not only to financial institutions and DNFBPs, but also to their directors and senior management.

rule: Countries should take immediate steps to become party to and implement fully the Vienna Convention, 1988; the Palermo Convention, 2000; the United Nations Convention against Corruption, 2003; and the Terrorist Financing Convention, 1999.
nlc: Verify that countries have become party to and have fully implemented the Vienna Convention, 1988; the Palermo Convention, 2000; the United Nations Convention against Corruption, 2003; and the Terrorist Financing Convention, 1999.

rule: Where applicable, countries are also encouraged to ratify and implement other relevant international conventions, such as the Council of Europe Convention on Cybercrime, 2001; the Inter-American Convention against Terrorism, 2002; and the Council of Europe Convention on Laundering, Search, Seizure and Confiscation of the Proceeds from Crime and on the Financing of Terrorism, 2005.
nlc: Verify that, where applicable, countries have ratified and implemented other relevant international conventions, such as the Council of Europe Convention on Cybercrime, 2001; the Inter-American Convention against Terrorism, 2002; and the Council of Europe Convention on Laundering, Search, Seizure and Confiscation of the Proceeds from Crime and on the Financing of Terrorism, 2005.

rule: Countries should rapidly, constructively and effectively provide the widest possible range of mutual legal assistance in relation to money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing investigations, prosecutions, and related proceedings.
nlc: Verify that countries rapidly, constructively and effectively provide the widest possible range of mutual legal assistance in relation to money laundering, associated predicate offences and terrorist financing investigations, prosecutions, and related proceedings.

rule: Countries should have an adequate legal basis for providing assistance and, where appropriate, should have in place treaties, arrangements or other mechanisms to enhance cooperation.
nlc: Verify that each country has an adequate legal basis for providing assistance and, where appropriate, has in place treaties, arrangements or other mechanisms to enhance cooperation.

rule: In particular, countries should: (a) Not prohibit, or place unreasonable or unduly restrictive conditions on, the provision of mutual legal assistance.
nlc: Verify that countries do not prohibit, or place unreasonable or unduly restrictive conditions on, the provision of mutual legal assistance.

rule: Ensure that they have clear and efficient processes for the timely prioritisation and execution of mutual legal assistance requests.
nlc: Verify that there are clear and efficient processes for the timely prioritisation and execution of mutual legal assistance requests.

rule: Countries should use a central authority, or another established official mechanism, for effective transmission and execution of requests.
nlc: Verify that countries use a central authority, or another established official mechanism, for effective transmission and execution of requests.

rule: To monitor progress on requests, a case management system should be maintained.
nlc: Verify that a case management system is maintained to monitor progress on requests.

rule: Not refuse to execute a request for mutual legal assistance on the sole ground that the offence is also considered to involve fiscal matters.
nlc: Verify that a request for mutual legal assistance is not refused on the sole ground that the offence is also considered to involve fiscal matters.

rule: Not refuse to execute a request for mutual legal assistance on the grounds that laws require financial institutions or DNFBPs to maintain secrecy or confidentiality (except where the relevant information that is sought is held in circumstances where legal professional privilege or legal professional secrecy applies).
